CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): Overall the paper is reasonably well organized (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated up front (end of Introduction). The method and algorithm pseudocode are clearly presented (Section 3 and Algorithm 1) and the experiments section describes setup and results in a logical way. However, clarity is reduced by repeated/duplicated paragraphs and blocks of text (large repetitions in the Appendix and near the end), a few grammatical/word-choice errors (e.g., “unfordable”), and some inconsistent notation/formatting in indices and subscripts (e.g., sti vs s_ti, at+1j), which make parts harder to follow; figures/tables are referenced but not shown in the provided text. | B(3): Overall the paper is reasonably well organized (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early in a clear bullet list. The method and Algorithm 1 are presented clearly (state function χ, actor πθ, critic Qϕ) and the experimental setup is described in adequate detail. However the manuscript contains distracting copy‑paste repetitions and formatting artifacts (many paragraphs repeated verbatim), some typos/wording problems (e.g. “unfordable”), and minor notation inconsistencies (ω vs w, occasional punctuation/citation clutter). Figures and tables are repeatedly referenced in the text but their content is not visible here, which reduces readability; these presentation issues make some sections harder to follow and require cleanup.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is reasonably well organized (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated up front (end of Introduction). The method and algorithm pseudocode are clearly presented (Section 3 and Algorithm 1) and the experiments section describes setup and results in a logical way. However, clarity is reduced by repeated/duplicated paragraphs and blocks of text (large repetitions in the Appendix and near the end), a few grammatical/word-choice errors (e.g., “unfordable”), and some inconsistent notation/formatting in indices and subscripts (e.g., sti vs s_ti, at+1j), which make parts harder to follow; figures/tables are referenced but not shown in the provided text.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is reasonably well organized (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early in a clear bullet list. The method and Algorithm 1 are presented clearly (state function χ, actor πθ, critic Qϕ) and the experimental setup is described in adequate detail. However the manuscript contains distracting copy‑paste repetitions and formatting artifacts (many paragraphs repeated verbatim), some typos/wording problems (e.g. “unfordable”), and minor notation inconsistencies (ω vs w, occasional punctuation/citation clutter). Figures and tables are repeatedly referenced in the text but their content is not visible here, which reduces readability; these presentation issues make some sections harder to follow and require cleanup.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies a known RL paradigm (actor-critic) to the problem of learning SGD step sizes (Section 3 and the stated contributions), which is a natural but not radical shift from existing adaptive optimizers and prior automatic step-size work (e.g., Schaul et al. 2013 and Daniel et al. 2016 cited in Section 2). The main novel elements are framing learning-rate control explicitly as an actor-critic RL task, using a sequence model (LSTM) as the actor, and the practical trick of feeding different samples to actor and critic to improve generalization; these are reasonable engineering/extensions but represent an incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new method. Therefore the contribution is moderately novel (incremental) rather than highly original. | B(3): The paper applies established actor–critic RL methods to the well-studied task of learning-rate control, formulating learning-rate scheduling as a sequential decision problem and using a critic to capture long-term reward (Sections 1–3). This framing and the practical trick of feeding different examples to actor and critic to improve generalization (Section 3.4) are modestly novel relative to prior adaptive step-size work (e.g. Schaul et al. 2013) which the authors discuss. However, the core components (actor–critic, LSTM policy, TD learning) are existing techniques and the contribution reads as a non‑trivial but largely incremental application/combination rather than a fundamentally new method.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a known RL paradigm (actor-critic) to the problem of learning SGD step sizes (Section 3 and the stated contributions), which is a natural but not radical shift from existing adaptive optimizers and prior automatic step-size work (e.g., Schaul et al. 2013 and Daniel et al. 2016 cited in Section 2). The main novel elements are framing learning-rate control explicitly as an actor-critic RL task, using a sequence model (LSTM) as the actor, and the practical trick of feeding different samples to actor and critic to improve generalization; these are reasonable engineering/extensions but represent an incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new method. Therefore the contribution is moderately novel (incremental) rather than highly original.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies established actor–critic RL methods to the well-studied task of learning-rate control, formulating learning-rate scheduling as a sequential decision problem and using a critic to capture long-term reward (Sections 1–3). This framing and the practical trick of feeding different examples to actor and critic to improve generalization (Section 3.4) are modestly novel relative to prior adaptive step-size work (e.g. Schaul et al. 2013) which the authors discuss. However, the core components (actor–critic, LSTM policy, TD learning) are existing techniques and the contribution reads as a non‑trivial but largely incremental application/combination rather than a fundamentally new method.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper's main claim (an actor–critic can learn per-step SGD learning rates and improve test performance) is supported by experiments on MNIST/CIFAR-10 versus common baselines, but experimental details are sparse (only 5 runs averaged, no statistical tests) and computational costs/hyperparameter sensitivity are not reported. The mathematical formulation is plausible and the TD / deterministic policy-gradient updates are consistent with standard actor–critic/DPG (Equations 6–9), but important assumptions are unstated: using a single scalar state (average minibatch loss) is not justified, and the practice of training the critic on one sample xi while updating the actor on a different xj (Sec. 3.3–3.4) breaks the usual on‑policy assumptions and is not theoretically justified. Overall the contribution is empirically suggestive but has methodological gaps and unsupported assumptions that prevent a higher technical-score. | B(3): The paper’s main claims (an actor–critic controller for learning rates, exploitation of long-term rewards, and improved generalization via separate actor/critic inputs) are evaluated with experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 and receive some empirical support (reported lower test error, robustness to overfitting). The maths are standard RL formulas (TD target, deterministic policy gradient) but contain notational/inconsistency issues (e.g. the actor gradient uses st+1 vs st and the ‘|a=πθ(s)’ notation is confusing), and no formal convergence or correctness arguments are given. The experimental methodology includes reasonable baselines and hyperparameter sweeps but omits statistical significance, and the key design choice—feeding different examples to actor and critic—breaks the usual on‑policy justification for the presented updates and is not theoretically justified (only heuristically argued). Overall the results partially justify the conclusions but important theoretical gaps and limited experimental rigor remain.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claim (an actor–critic can learn per-step SGD learning rates and improve test performance) is supported by experiments on MNIST/CIFAR-10 versus common baselines, but experimental details are sparse (only 5 runs averaged, no statistical tests) and computational costs/hyperparameter sensitivity are not reported. The mathematical formulation is plausible and the TD / deterministic policy-gradient updates are consistent with standard actor–critic/DPG (Equations 6–9), but important assumptions are unstated: using a single scalar state (average minibatch loss) is not justified, and the practice of training the critic on one sample xi while updating the actor on a different xj (Sec. 3.3–3.4) breaks the usual on‑policy assumptions and is not theoretically justified. Overall the contribution is empirically suggestive but has methodological gaps and unsupported assumptions that prevent a higher technical-score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims (an actor–critic controller for learning rates, exploitation of long-term rewards, and improved generalization via separate actor/critic inputs) are evaluated with experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 and receive some empirical support (reported lower test error, robustness to overfitting). The maths are standard RL formulas (TD target, deterministic policy gradient) but contain notational/inconsistency issues (e.g. the actor gradient uses st+1 vs st and the ‘|a=πθ(s)’ notation is confusing), and no formal convergence or correctness arguments are given. The experimental methodology includes reasonable baselines and hyperparameter sweeps but omits statistical significance, and the key design choice—feeding different examples to actor and critic—breaks the usual on‑policy justification for the presented updates and is not theoretically justified (only heuristically argued). Overall the results partially justify the conclusions but important theoretical gaps and limited experimental rigor remain.
